title: Sarah J. — USA → Yongin
body:
## Sarah J. — From Virginia, USA to Yongin, South Korea

> "BRIDGE made the entire process seamless. From my first inquiry to settling into my apartment in Yongin, I never felt lost or unsupported."

### My Background

I graduated from the University of Virginia with a degree in English Literature in 2023. I had always been curious about living abroad, and teaching English in Korea seemed like the perfect way to combine my love of language with international experience. I had no prior teaching experience, but I did complete a 120-hour online TEFL certificate before applying.

### How BRIDGE Helped

I found BRIDGE through a recommendation from a friend who had taught in Seoul. From the start, they were incredibly responsive. Within a week of submitting my resume, I had three school options to consider. My BRIDGE coordinator helped me compare the positions — explaining the differences in school culture, student age groups, and neighborhoods — so I could make an informed decision.

The visa process was the part I dreaded most, but BRIDGE walked me through every step. They told me exactly which FBI channeler to use, how to get my degree apostilled, and even reminded me about deadlines before documents expired. When my VIN arrived, they sent detailed instructions on what to bring to my embassy appointment.

### Life in Yongin

I chose a kindergarten and elementary hagwon in Yongin. The school provided a clean, furnished studio apartment about a 10-minute walk from work. Yongin is quieter than Seoul, but I love that — there are beautiful parks, Everland is nearby for weekends, and Seoul is just 40 minutes away by bus.

My students are energetic and hilarious. Teaching 5-year-olds was challenging at first, but the school provided a solid curriculum and my co-teachers have been incredibly supportive.

### My Advice

- **Start your documents early** — the FBI background check took longer than I expected
- **Be open-minded about location** — I initially wanted Seoul, but Yongin turned out to be perfect for me
- **Trust the process** — BRIDGE knows what they are doing. Ask questions, follow their timeline, and it all comes together

I am now in my second year and have already renewed my contract. Korea feels like home.

title: Rachel P. — New Zealand to Jeju
body:
# Rachel P. — New Zealand to Jeju Island

**Background**: 26, from Auckland. Bachelor's in Communications, 120-hour TEFL certificate. No prior teaching experience.

## Why I Chose Korea
I'd been working in marketing for two years and felt stuck. A friend who taught in Seoul kept posting these amazing travel photos and I thought — why not? I found BRIDGE through a Reddit thread and sent in my application on a whim.

## The Process
The BRIDGE team was incredibly responsive. Within a week of my application, I had a phone consultation where they asked about my preferences. I said I wanted somewhere with nature and a smaller community feel — they immediately suggested Jeju.

My interview with the school was smooth. The director was friendly, asked standard questions, and seemed genuinely interested in what I could bring to the table. BRIDGE prepped me beforehand so I knew what to expect.

Documents took about 6 weeks total (NZ police check + apostille + degree verification). BRIDGE walked me through every step and kept a shared checklist so nothing fell through the cracks.

## Life on Jeju
I teach at a small academy in Jeju City. My students are mostly elementary-aged and absolutely hilarious. The Korean co-teacher is wonderful and has become one of my closest friends here.

Jeju is stunning — I hike Hallasan on weekends, swim at hidden beaches, and the cost of living is lower than Auckland. My apartment overlooks tangerine orchards. I sometimes have to pinch myself.

## The Honest Parts
- I miss my family, especially during Korean holidays when everyone goes to their hometowns
- The language barrier was frustrating for the first two months
- My school expected more admin work than I anticipated
- But the support from BRIDGE made every challenge manageable

## Would I Recommend BRIDGE?
Absolutely. They weren't just a recruiter — they felt like a support system. Even months after I arrived, I could message them with questions and get a response within hours.

**Time in Korea**: 14 months and counting. Already renewed my contract.

title: Chris M. — USA to Daejeon
body:
# Chris M. — USA (Texas) to Daejeon

**Background**: 31, from Houston. Bachelor's in History, CELTA certified. 2 years teaching experience in Thailand.

## Switching from Thailand to Korea
I loved Thailand but wanted better salary and savings potential. Korea was the obvious choice — higher pay, free housing, and a strong education system. After some bad experiences with other recruiters, I found BRIDGE through ESL Cafe forums.

## Why BRIDGE Was Different
Most recruiters I'd dealt with before felt like used car salesmen — pushing any job to get their commission. BRIDGE actually listened. When I said I wanted a hagwon with maximum 25 teaching hours and a school that wouldn't pile on admin work, they found exactly that.

They were also transparent about what to expect. "This school is great but the director can be strict about punctuality" — that kind of honest heads-up is rare in this industry.

## Daejeon — The Underrated City
Seoul gets all the hype, but Daejeon is incredible for quality of life. It's smaller, cheaper, less crowded, and still has KTX access to Seoul in under an hour. The food is amazing, the people are friendly, and I have a spacious apartment that would cost triple in Gangnam.

## My School
I teach middle school students at an English academy. Classes are small (6-8 students), the curriculum is well-organized, and my Korean co-teacher handles all parent communication. I work 9:30 AM to 6:30 PM with genuine breaks.

## Finances
On a 2.7M salary with free housing:
- Monthly expenses: about 800K won
- Monthly savings: about 1.5M won (roughly $1,100 USD)
- Severance after year 1: 2.7M won bonus
- This is life-changing for someone who was living paycheck to paycheck in the US.

## Advice for Others
1. Don't limit yourself to Seoul — smaller cities offer better value
2. Trust your recruiter but verify everything in the contract
3. Bring a good attitude and be flexible
4. Korea rewards teachers who stay and build relationships

**Time in Korea**: 2 years. Planning to stay at least one more.

title: Michael T. — Canada → Daegu
body:
## Michael T. — From Toronto, Canada to Daegu, South Korea

> "I wanted to save money and experience real Korean culture, not just the Seoul bubble. BRIDGE found me the perfect position in Daegu, and I have never looked back."

### My Background

I grew up in Toronto and graduated from York University with a BA in Communications. After working in marketing for three years, I felt stuck in a routine and wanted something completely different. A college friend who had taught in Busan told me about the ESL industry in Korea, and that planted the seed.

I completed a 120-hour TEFL course online while still working my marketing job. I had zero classroom experience, but I figured my communication skills and energy would carry me. They did.

### Working with BRIDGE

I reached out to three different agencies. BRIDGE stood out because they actually listened to what I wanted. When I said I was interested in a smaller city where I could save more money and immerse myself in Korean culture, they did not try to push Seoul on me. Instead, they recommended Daegu and explained why it would be a great fit.

Within 10 days, I had two interview offers. My coordinator prepped me for each one — what the schools expected, what questions they would likely ask, and how to present my lack of experience as a strength (fresh perspective, high energy, willingness to learn).

The Canadian document process was straightforward. BRIDGE sent me a detailed checklist: RCMP criminal record check via the accredited agency, degree authentication through the Korean Consulate in Toronto, and the TEFL certificate. Everything was clearly laid out with timelines.

### Life in Daegu

I chose a hagwon in Suseong-gu, Daegu's education-heavy district. My students are elementary and middle school age, classes of about 8-12 kids. The school gave me a clean, recently renovated studio apartment about a 7-minute walk from work.

Daegu's cost of living is incredible. I pay nothing for rent (school-provided), and I spend maybe 600,000-700,000 KRW per month on everything else — food, transportation, phone, and going out. I have been consistently saving over 1,500,000 KRW per month.

The food scene here is underrated. Daegu is famous for its spicy dishes — makchang (grilled intestines) and napjak mandu (flat dumplings) are local specialties I have grown to love. Seomun Night Market is my weekend happy place.

### What Surprised Me

- How welcoming Koreans are when you make even a small effort to speak Korean
- How affordable everything is outside of Seoul
- How tight-knit the expat community is in Daegu — everyone knows everyone
- How easy it is to travel from Daegu — KTX to Seoul in under 2 hours, Busan in 45 minutes

### Advice for Canadians

- Start the RCMP check early — it took me about 3 weeks
- The Korean Consulate in Toronto is efficient but has specific hours — plan your visit
- Do not overlook smaller cities like Daegu — the quality of life and savings potential are hard to beat
- BRIDGE's post-arrival check-ins were genuinely helpful during my first month of adjustment

I am 8 months into my contract and have already told my school I want to renew. Daegu feels like home now.

title: Aoife K. — Ireland → Incheon
body:
## Aoife K. — From Dublin, Ireland to Incheon, South Korea

> "As an Irish applicant, I was not sure how familiar agencies would be with our specific document requirements. BRIDGE knew the Irish system inside and out."

### My Background

I am from Dublin and graduated from University College Dublin (UCD) with a BA in English and Sociology. I spent a year working at a language school in Madrid before deciding I wanted to try Asia. Korea appealed to me because of the salary, the structured work environment, and honestly — the food. I had been watching Korean cooking videos for years.

I already had my CELTA from my time preparing for Madrid, which gave me an advantage in the application process.

### The Irish-Specific Process

This is where BRIDGE really earned my trust. The Irish document process has some quirks that not every agency understands:

1. **Garda Vetting**: I needed a police clearance through the National Vetting Bureau. BRIDGE explained that I would need to apply through a registered organization (my TEFL provider helped with this). It took about 2 weeks.

2. **Department of Foreign Affairs Apostille**: My degree and Garda Vetting letter both needed apostilles. I went to the DFA office in Dublin in person — same-day service, which was brilliant.

3. **Korean Embassy Dublin**: Submitted my visa application in person. The embassy staff were helpful, and I had my E-2 visa within a week.

BRIDGE had a specific Ireland checklist that anticipated every step. They even knew about the Garda Vetting processing times and built that into my timeline.

### Why Incheon

I initially wanted Seoul, but BRIDGE suggested I consider Songdo in Incheon. They explained that Songdo offered a modern living environment, higher-than-average salary for the area, and easy access to Seoul by subway. After researching it, I was convinced.

My school is a well-established English kindergarten and elementary hagwon in Songdo International City. The facilities are impressive — smart boards in every classroom, a small library, and a rooftop play area for the younger students.

### Life in Songdo

Songdo is unlike anywhere else in Korea. It feels almost futuristic — wide boulevards, modern architecture, Central Park (yes, they named it after the one in New York), and everything is clean and well-maintained.

**What I love:**
- My apartment has a view of the park and is a 10-minute walk to work
- The area is safe, quiet, and perfect for someone who values peace during the week
- Seoul is 40 minutes away by subway for weekend adventures
- Incheon Airport is 20 minutes away — I have done weekend trips to Japan and Taiwan
- Triple Street Mall and NC Cube Canal Walk are right in Songdo for shopping and food

**What took adjustment:**
- Songdo can feel a bit sterile compared to the energy of older Korean neighborhoods
- The expat community is smaller here, though it is growing
- You need to go to Bupyeong or Seoul for a proper night out

### My Teaching Experience

I teach morning kindergarten classes (ages 5-7) and afternoon elementary classes (ages 8-12). The kindergarten work is exhausting but rewarding — lots of songs, crafts, and storytelling. The afternoon classes are more structured with textbooks and writing exercises.

My co-teachers have been wonderful. They helped me navigate everything from setting up my phone plan to ordering at restaurants. Korean workplace culture values relationships, and making an effort to join team dinners (회식) goes a long way.

### Advice for Irish Teachers

- Start Garda Vetting as early as possible — it is the slowest step
- The DFA in-person apostille service in Dublin is fast and worth the trip
- Consider Incheon seriously — it is not just "Seoul's airport city" anymore
- BRIDGE's knowledge of the Irish system saved me weeks of confusion
- Bring a good rain jacket — Korean summers are as wet as Irish ones

I am halfway through my contract and already planning year two. Korea has exceeded every expectation.

title: James W. — Australia → Jeju
body:
## James W. — From Melbourne, Australia to Jeju Island, South Korea

> "I told BRIDGE I wanted somewhere with nature, beaches, and a slower pace of life. They said two words: Jeju Island. Best decision I have ever made."

### My Background

I am from Melbourne and graduated from Monash University with a Bachelor of Education. I spent two years teaching primary school in Victoria before deciding I wanted an international experience. I had traveled through Southeast Asia and Japan, but Korea was not originally on my radar until a mate who had taught in Daejeon raved about it.

Having a teaching degree gave me more options than the typical TEFL-only applicant. BRIDGE explained that some schools, especially international schools and higher-end hagwons, specifically prefer candidates with formal education qualifications.

### The Australian Process

Australian documents for the E-2 visa are relatively straightforward:

1. **AFP National Police Check**: Applied online through the Australian Federal Police website. Cost about AUD 42 and took 10 business days.

2. **DFAT Apostille**: Sent my degree and police check to the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade for apostilles. I used the mail service — it took about 5 business days.

3. **Korean Consulate Melbourne**: Submitted my visa application. Processing took about a week.

BRIDGE provided an Australia-specific guide that made the whole process smooth. They warned me that the AFP check can sometimes take longer if you have lived at multiple addresses, so I applied early.

### Choosing Jeju

When I told BRIDGE I wanted nature and a relaxed lifestyle, they immediately mentioned Jeju. I was hesitant at first — an island seemed isolated. But they explained the flight connections (hourly flights to Seoul, cheap as 30,000 KRW), the growing expat community, and the outdoor lifestyle. As someone who grew up surfing at Torquay, the idea of living on a volcanic island with beaches sold me.

I ended up at an English academy in Jeju City with a mix of elementary and middle school students.

### Island Life

Jeju is genuinely special. After living in Melbourne — a great city, but still a city — Jeju feels like a permanent holiday.

**My weekly routine:**
- Monday to Friday: Teach 1:00 PM - 9:00 PM (afternoon/evening hagwon schedule)
- Saturday morning: Surf at Jungmun Beach or hike a section of the Olle Trail
- Sunday: Explore a new cafe, drive to a waterfall, or just rest

**What makes Jeju unique:**
- I bought a used car for 3,000,000 KRW — essential on the island, but driving here is easy and scenic
- Hallasan hike is incredible — did it in autumn with the foliage and it was one of the best days of my life
- The seafood is absurdly fresh. Haenyeo (women divers) sell their catch right on the coast
- Jeju has its own distinct culture, dialect, and traditions that feel separate from mainland Korea
- The weather is milder than mainland — I barely needed a heavy coat in winter

**The challenges:**
- Public transport is limited — you really need a car or scooter
- The expat community is small (but very friendly — everyone becomes mates quickly)
- Shopping options are fewer than Seoul, but online delivery works perfectly
- Summer monsoon season is intense — heavy rain and occasional typhoons

### Teaching on Jeju

My school has about 80 students across different levels. Classes are small (6-10 students), which I love after teaching classes of 25+ in Australia. The curriculum is textbook-based but I have freedom to add my own activities. The school director values creativity and has given me space to try new approaches.

The students are curious about Australia and constantly ask me about kangaroos, spiders, and whether I have seen a shark. It never gets old.

### Advice for Australians

- The AFP check is easy — apply online and it arrives digitally. Print it and get it apostilled.
- If you have a teaching degree, mention it prominently — it opens more doors
- Jeju is not for everyone — if you need busy nightlife and shopping, choose Seoul or Busan
- But if you love nature, ocean, hiking, and a relaxed community — Jeju is paradise
- BRIDGE helped me find a car, set up insurance, and navigate the Jeju-specific quirks. Their support did not end at placement.

I am extending for a second year. Melbourne will always be home, but Jeju has my heart for now.

title: Hannah R. — New Zealand → Gwangju
body:
## Hannah R. — From Auckland, New Zealand to Gwangju, South Korea

> "Gwangju was not on my radar at all. BRIDGE suggested it based on what I was looking for — an artsy city with affordable living and a strong community feel. They were spot on."

### My Background

I grew up in Auckland and studied at the University of Auckland, graduating with a BA in Linguistics and Korean Studies. Yes, I was already studying Korean before I came — I got into K-dramas during university and it spiraled into a genuine academic interest in the language and culture.

I completed a 120-hour TEFL certificate and had done some private English tutoring in Auckland, but no formal classroom experience. My Korean language ability (intermediate level, TOPIK 3) turned out to be a significant advantage in the application process.

### The New Zealand Process

New Zealand's document requirements are similar to Australia's:

1. **NZ Police Vetting**: Applied through the Ministry of Justice website. Cost NZD 10 and arrived in about a week. Very straightforward.

2. **MFAT Apostille**: Sent my degree and police clearance to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade. Processing took about 5 business days by mail.

3. **Korean Embassy Wellington**: Submitted my visa application. Received my E-2 visa within a week.

BRIDGE's New Zealand-specific guide was concise and accurate. The NZ process is one of the simpler ones among the eligible countries, which was a relief.

### Why Gwangju

I told BRIDGE I wanted:
- A city with culture and art (not just a generic suburban sprawl)
- Affordable living so I could save and travel
- A place where my Korean skills would be useful (fewer English speakers = more practice)
- Something different from what other New Zealanders typically choose

They recommended Gwangju — South Korea's cultural capital, known for its arts scene, democratic history, and incredible food. I had never considered it, but after reading about it, I was sold.

### Life in Gwangju

Gwangju is a city of about 1.4 million people in the southwestern Jeolla Province. It is often overlooked by ESL teachers who default to Seoul or Busan, and that is part of its charm.

**The food**: Jeolla Province is considered the culinary heartland of Korea. Gwangju takes this seriously. A standard 7,000 KRW meal at a local restaurant comes with 8-12 side dishes (반찬). I have never eaten this well in my life. Local specialties include:
- Yukhoe (육회) — Korean beef tartare
- Tteokgalbi (떡갈비) — minced beef rib patties (Damyang style)
- Hongeo (홍어) — fermented skate (an acquired taste, but a rite of passage)

**The art scene**: Gwangju hosts the **Gwangju Biennale**, one of Asia's most prestigious contemporary art exhibitions. The city is dotted with galleries, street art, and creative spaces. The **Asia Culture Center** is a massive cultural complex in the city center.

**Historical significance**: Gwangju is the site of the **May 18th Democratic Uprising** (1980), a pivotal moment in Korea's democratization. The memorial and cemetery are powerful places to visit. The city has a strong sense of civic pride rooted in this history.

**Cost of living**: Gwangju is one of the most affordable major cities in Korea.
- My rent (school-provided studio): Free
- Monthly expenses: 500,000-600,000 KRW
- Monthly savings: 1,500,000+ KRW

### My School

I teach at a mid-sized hagwon near Chonnam National University. My students range from 3rd grade to 6th grade elementary. The school values speaking practice, so my classes are conversation-heavy with games, role-plays, and presentations.

My Korean language skills have been a huge asset. I can communicate with Korean co-teachers easily, understand parent feedback, and even explain grammar concepts by comparing Korean and English structures. Several parents specifically requested me because I could bridge the gap.

### The Expat Community

Gwangju's foreigner community is small — maybe a few hundred ESL teachers — but incredibly welcoming. There is a weekly language exchange at a cafe near Chonnam University, regular hiking trips to Mudeungsan Mountain, and a surprisingly active social scene concentrated around the Chungjangno area downtown.

### Advice for New Zealanders

- The NZ police and apostille process is among the easiest — take advantage of that and apply early
- If you have any Korean language ability, mention it to BRIDGE — it significantly expands your options
- Do not sleep on Gwangju — the food alone is worth it, and the quality of life is outstanding
- Bring a few comforts from home — Whittaker's chocolate is good currency for making Korean friends
- BRIDGE connected me with another Kiwi teacher in Gwangju before I arrived, which made the transition much smoother

I am nearing the end of my first year and have already signed a renewal. Gwangju is a hidden gem, and I am glad BRIDGE pointed me toward it.

title: Tyler M. — USA → Daejeon
body:
## Tyler M. — From Chicago, USA to Daejeon, South Korea

> "I chose Daejeon because BRIDGE explained it was the perfect base for exploring all of Korea. They were right — I have visited more of this country in 6 months than some people do in 2 years."

### My Background

I am from the Chicago suburbs and graduated from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign with a BS in Psychology. After graduation, I worked as a behavioral therapist for a year, but the pay was low and the burnout was real. A former colleague who had taught in Korea told me it was the best decision he ever made. That was enough for me.

I got my TEFL certification through a reputable online program (150 hours with a practicum component) and started looking at agencies. I applied to four. BRIDGE was the only one that asked me detailed questions about what kind of school environment I wanted, what age group I preferred, and what my goals were beyond just "living in Korea."

### The American Process

As an American, the E-2 visa process involves a few specific steps:

1. **FBI Background Check**: I used the FBI-approved channeler (Fieldprint/Idemia) for faster processing. Mailed my fingerprint card in, and got results in about 4 weeks. BRIDGE warned me this was the longest step and told me to start immediately — good advice.

2. **State Department Apostille**: Sent my degree and FBI check to the U.S. Department of State for apostilles. Used a document service BRIDGE recommended, which handled everything for about $200. Took about 2 weeks.

3. **Korean Consulate Chicago**: Submitted my visa application. Turnaround was about a week.

BRIDGE's American checklist was thorough. They even included tips about which FBI channeler to use and how to avoid common mistakes that cause delays.

### Why Daejeon

My main priorities were:
- Save as much money as possible (student loans waiting back home)
- Be able to travel easily around Korea on weekends
- Avoid the intensity of Seoul but still have urban amenities

BRIDGE suggested Daejeon because of its central location (50 minutes to Seoul by KTX), affordable cost of living, and the presence of KAIST and other universities that create a younger, more international atmosphere in the Yuseong area.

I was matched with a hagwon in Seo-gu that teaches elementary through middle school students. The salary is 2,300,000 KRW per month with a rent-free furnished apartment.

### Life in Daejeon

Daejeon has become my ideal base camp for experiencing Korea.

**My travel record in 6 months:**
- Seoul (8 times — concerts, museums, nightlife)
- Busan (3 times — beaches, BIFF, Jagalchi Market)
- Gyeongju (twice — the ancient capital, incredible temples)
- Jeonju (once — bibimbap heaven, hanok village)
- Sokcho/Seoraksan (once — best hiking I have ever done)
- DMZ Tour (once — surreal and unforgettable)

All of these trips started from Daejeon Station, usually for under 30,000 KRW by KTX or express bus. Being in the center of the country makes everywhere accessible.

**Daily life in Daejeon:**
- My apartment is a 5-minute walk from work — no commute
- I spend about 700,000 KRW per month total (food, transport, phone, entertainment)
- Seongsimdang bakery is a Daejeon institution — their Twigim Soboro bread is legendary
- Yuseong Hot Springs area has free foot baths and a relaxing vibe
- Gyejoksan barefoot trail is my go-to weekend morning walk
- The Daejeon expat group on social media organizes events almost weekly

### My Teaching Experience

I teach 6 classes per day, each 40 minutes, Monday through Friday. My students are great — curious, energetic, and often hilarious. The curriculum is structured, but my director encourages me to add my own style. I use a lot of games and competitive activities, which works well with the Korean students' naturally competitive spirit.

My psychology background has actually been surprisingly useful in the classroom. Understanding child development and behavioral patterns helps me manage classes and connect with students who might be struggling or acting out.

### Financial Snapshot

| Item | Monthly |
|------|--------|
| Salary | 2,300,000 KRW |
| Rent | 0 (provided) |
| Food | 350,000 KRW |
| Transportation | 80,000 KRW |
| Phone | 35,000 KRW |
| Entertainment/Travel | 200,000 KRW |
| **Total savings** | **~1,600,000 KRW (~$1,200 USD)** |

After 6 months, I have saved enough to pay off a significant chunk of my student loans while still traveling extensively.

### Advice for Americans

- Start the FBI check **the day you decide to come to Korea** — it is the bottleneck
- Use a document service for the State Department apostille unless you are near D.C.
- Daejeon is the best-kept secret in the Korean ESL world — central, affordable, livable
- BRIDGE was honest about every school they recommended. When I asked about a position they had concerns about, they told me straight up instead of pushing it
- Bring your favorite snacks from home — Korean convenience stores are amazing, but sometimes you just want Flamin' Hot Cheetos

I am renewing my contract next month. The plan is two years total, enough to clear my loans and build savings. BRIDGE made getting here easy. Staying was my own choice — and an easy one.

title: David N. — South Africa → Bundang
body:
## David N. — From Cape Town, South Africa to Bundang, South Korea

> "As a South African, I was worried the visa process would be more complicated for me. BRIDGE knew exactly what was needed and guided me through it without any issues."

### My Background

I am from Cape Town and graduated from the University of Cape Town with a BA in Linguistics. After working in customer service for two years, I wanted a career change and an adventure. South Korea was not my first thought — I had considered Japan and Thailand — but the salary and benefits package for ESL teachers in Korea was significantly better.

### The Application Process

I applied through BRIDGE's website on a Wednesday evening. By Friday morning, I had a response with a detailed explanation of what documents I would need as a South African citizen. The criminal background check process from SAPS (South African Police Service) was straightforward once I knew the steps.

The key challenge for South Africans is the **consular legalization** process, since our documents need authentication through the Department of International Relations (DIRCO) before the Korean Embassy can process them. BRIDGE had a clear checklist that saved me from making costly mistakes.

### Finding the Right Position

BRIDGE matched me with three positions in the Gyeonggi-do area. I chose a well-established hagwon in Bundang because of the higher salary, modern facilities, and the area's reputation as a great place to live.

During my video interview, my BRIDGE coordinator joined the call to help with any communication gaps. The school director was warm and professional, and I felt confident accepting the offer.

### Life in Bundang

Bundang is a modern new city that feels like a cleaner, more spacious version of Seoul. My apartment is in a newer building with a gym and rooftop garden. The Shinbundang Line gets me to Gangnam Station in 15 minutes.

What surprised me most was how welcoming Koreans are. My co-workers invited me to dinners, helped me set up my bank account, and even taught me basic Korean phrases for daily life.

### Tips for South African Applicants

- Start your SAPS background check **immediately** — it can take 2-3 weeks
- Budget for DIRCO authentication fees and courier costs
- BRIDGE understands the SA-specific requirements — lean on their expertise
- Bundang and the greater Gyeonggi area are underrated — do not fixate on Seoul

title: Emma L. — UK → Seoul
body:
## Emma L. — From Manchester, UK to Seoul, South Korea

> "I had my CELTA and two years of experience teaching in Vietnam, but navigating the Korean system on my own seemed daunting. BRIDGE simplified everything and found me a position that matched exactly what I was looking for."

### My Background

I did my undergraduate degree in History at the University of Manchester, followed by a CELTA at International House in Barcelona. After that, I spent two years teaching at a language center in Ho Chi Minh City. I loved Southeast Asia, but I wanted a change — higher salary, four distinct seasons, and a new cultural challenge. Korea ticked all the boxes.

### Why I Chose BRIDGE

I contacted several agencies before settling on BRIDGE. What set them apart was transparency. They did not oversell positions or pressure me into making quick decisions. When I asked tough questions about working hours, overtime expectations, and school turnover rates, they gave honest answers instead of glossing over potential issues.

### The UK-Specific Process

As a British citizen, I needed:

1. **ACRO Criminal Record Check** — Applied online, received in about two weeks
2. **FCDO Apostille** — I used the premium same-day service in London (worth the trip from Manchester)
3. **KVAC London** — Submitted my visa application in person; received my passport back with the E-2 sticker within a week

BRIDGE provided a step-by-step timeline specific to UK applicants, which was invaluable. I have heard horror stories of people getting documents in the wrong order and having to start over — that was never a risk with BRIDGE's guidance.

### My Position in Seoul

I teach at a mid-sized hagwon in Mapo-gu, near Hongdae. My students are elementary-aged (8-13 Korean age), and classes are small — usually 6-10 students. The curriculum is textbook-based but I have freedom to supplement with my own activities and games.

**What I love:**
- The neighborhood — Hongdae and Hapjeong are vibrant, full of cafes and indie shops
- My apartment — small but well-located, 5-minute walk to the subway
- The food — Korean BBQ, tteokbokki, and the bakeries are incredible
- Weekend trips — Busan by KTX in 2.5 hours, hiking in Bukhansan

### Advice for UK Teachers

- The **CELTA** opens doors — if you do not have one, at least get a reputable 120-hour TEFL
- Use the **FCDO premium service** if you can get to London — waiting weeks for an apostille by post is stressful
- Seoul is expensive but worth it if you value culture, food, and nightlife
- BRIDGE's post-arrival support was a bonus I did not expect — they checked in on me during my first month and helped resolve a minor housing issue quickly

I am halfway through my first year and already planning to stay for a second. If you are a UK teacher considering Korea, I cannot recommend BRIDGE enough.
